summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim parenting arrangements – Where there are two children to the marriage – Where the father seeks unsupervised time with one of the children and for family therapy to commence with the other – Orders made for the continuation of supervised time and for the father to undergo psychological treatment.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

This matter concerns the applicant father, Mr Aliye born in 1985 (“the father”) and the respondent mother, Ms Salomea born in 1989 (“the mother”) in regards to the interim parenting arrangements for the children, X born in 2020 and Y born in 2022 (“the children”).

BACKGROUND

The father commenced these proceedings on 27 March 2025 seeking orders that the mother return the children to Australia. This was within the context of an escalation in conflict between the parties where the husband travelled to the United Kingdom from India, on his case, intending to return to Australia. There were legal proceedings in the United Kingdom and the father was eventually arrested. The children returned to Australia and orders were made for them to live with mother and for one of the children to spend supervised time with the father. The father now seeks, at this interim stage, that such time becomes unsupervised and family therapy commences with the child who he is not spending time with.

RELEVANT LAW

In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to the children, I must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.[1]
Section 60CC(2) of the Act sets out the list of matters that the Court must consider in determining what is in the children's best interests.
Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B). The objects of Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (“the Act”) are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child of 20 November 1989.[2] My decision is guided by those objects.

CONSIDERATION

Having regard to all of the circumstances, I find it appropriate that supervised time continues and the father is to obtain directed psychological treatment and provide evidence of such. I find this for several reasons.
In her most recent affidavit of 23 January 2026, the mother provides a more detailed account of the family violence that occurred in the household, and more specifically, going to the father's conduct towards his son, who was at that time four years of age. A video of the father and the child shows that the father's behaviour was uncontrolled and showed clearly excessive discipline of the child. He raised his voice, he hit the child quite strongly in an angry forceful way and subjected the child to ongoing verbal abuse of a severe kind. As described in the affidavit, it was not on one occasion, but on several occasions, and the treatment of the child was totally inappropriate.
The father has accepted that he has subjected the mother and the children to family violence, but my concern is that the limited evidence before the Court as to his engagement with a psychologist does not give any indication that he is seeking to address the particular issues that arise as a result of the evidence of the mother.
The Court is not prepared to make an order for unsupervised time until the father has provided evidence to the Court of a directed and concerted approach to deal with the issues which arise from the evidence of the mother, and, for those reasons, the Court makes orders for his treating psychologist to provide a detailed treatment plan that addresses the issues of family violence, his discipline of the children and his anger management. The father is facing criminal charges, and I will not go beyond what is necessary to provide reasons for the judgment so as not to prejudice any criminal proceedings that he faces.
